title: God Is King Over Words
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: God is King Over Words - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 1_7-22.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Communication Has Broken Down Everywhere
subpoints: We live in the age of the absurd: no matter what you say, someone somewhere is offended
Words are incredibly powerful: language is not a human invention but a characteristic of God Himself
Language predates the fall—God spoke to Adam before sin entered
Satan used the misuse of language to tempt Eve: “Did God really say?” (Genesis 3)
Communication problems are not primarily word problems but heart problems (Matthew 12; Proverbs 4:23)

outline.title: Statement 1: God Is Lord of Lords and King of Kings
subpoints: No one challenges, advises, or can stand in the way of God’s will (Daniel 4: Nebuchadnezzar’s testimony)
We will never be in a situation where God is not ruling
Knowing God is in control allows us not to give in to panic or anxiety
We can pay attention to what God calls us to do, speaking for His glory in any situation

outline.title: Statement 2: God Rules for the Benefit of His Church
subpoints: God works all things in history to gather His people to Himself (Ephesians 1:7-12)
Jesus Christ is the Head of the church today—not only in the future
How much of our communication involves complaining about circumstances God has ordained?
True praise is not limited to moments we decide are good—God is faithful to His church in all circumstances

outline.title: Statement 3: God Rules Over the Specific Details of Our Lives
subpoints: God is not only sovereign globally—He is sovereign over individual circumstances (Acts 17)
God appointed the place where you live and the family you are in so you can reach out to Him
Communication problems often focus on people around us, but God is using those circumstances as His tools
God is near us—this should shape how we speak even in conflict

outline.title: Statements 4-5: God Rules Over Our Salvation and Our Sanctification
subpoints: God is sovereign over every aspect of salvation: He chose us, predestined us, adopted us (Ephesians 1:3-6)
True worship begins when we grasp sovereign grace; pride gives way to praise
Every word we speak should reflect awareness of our complete dependence on God
God rules circumstances for our sanctification—complaining about our circumstances is complaining at God’s plan (James 1)

outline.title: Statements 6-7: God Rules Over Relationships and Over All Things for His Glory
subpoints: People in our lives are placed there by God to shape us into the image of Christ
We treat people as irritants, but God sends them as agents of our sanctification
God is not working for our temporal happiness—He is working for His own glory through us
Every word should bring God the glory He deserves and redemptive good to those around us

summary:
In this sermon from Ephesians 1, Pastor Tito Lyro addresses the breakdown of communication in contemporary society and traces its root cause not to words themselves but to the heart. After reading Ephesians 1:3-12, he observes that language predates the fall—God spoke with Adam before sin entered—and that Satan introduced sin into the world through the deliberate misuse of language (“Did God really say?”). The contemporary crisis of communication, where every statement offends someone and words no longer carry agreed meanings, is therefore not primarily a cultural or technological problem but a spiritual one rooted in unsubmitted hearts.

The sermon's body presents seven interconnected statements about God's sovereignty and how each applies to communication. God is Lord of Lords and King of Kings: we will never be in a situation where God is not ruling, so we need not panic or grasp for control in our speech. God rules all things for the benefit of His church: knowing this should transform our complaints about circumstances into praise, and our anxiety into rest. God rules over the specific details of individual lives: He appointed where we live and the families we are in (Acts 17), so the irritating people around us are not accidental. God rules over every aspect of salvation (Ephesians 1:3-6): grasping this displaces human pride with worship, and the words that flow from a worshipful heart will be marked by humility rather than self-justification. God rules circumstances for our sanctification: when we complain about our trials, we are essentially grumbling against God's plan to make us more like Christ.

The final two statements bring the series to a practical focus. God rules over relationships for our sanctification: the untimed phone call, the demanding relative, the unthankful child are all tools in God's hands, not obstacles to be removed. God rules all things for His glory (Ephesians 1:12): life is not about personal comfort or a positive self-image but about being lights that shine in darkness. Every word we speak must meet two standards—it must bring God the glory He deserves, and it must bring redemptive good to those who hear it. The sermon closes with seven searching self-examination questions about whether our speech reflects rest or wrestling with God's sovereignty.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Ephesians 1:3-12

Please, may, stand if you're able. And for our scripture reading this morning, we're going to turn to the first chapter of the book of Ephesians. We're going to read verses three through twelve of chapter one.

*Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ, just as He chose us in Him before the foundation of the world that we should be holy and without blame before Him in love, having predestined us to adoption as sons by Jesus Christ to Himself, according to the good pleasure of His will, to the praise of the glory of His grace, by which He made us accepted in the Beloved. In Him we have redemption through His blood, the forgiveness of sins, according to the riches of His grace, which He made to abound to us in all wisdom and prudence, who has made known to us the mystery of His will, according to His good pleasure, which He purposed in Himself. That in the dispensation of the fullness of the times, He might gather together in one all things in Christ, both which are in heaven and which are on earth. In Him also we have obtained an inheritance, being predestined according to the purpose of Him who works all things according to the counsel of His will, that we who first trusted in Christ should be to the praise of His glory.*

This is the word of our Lord. May He bless it to our hearts. Let us pray together.

*Father, we pray that You'd speak to our hearts even as we consider Your word. We pray Father, renew our minds, that we might follow Your faith, we pray in Jesus' name. Amen.*

Please be seated.

## Introduction: The Age of Absurd Communication

Have you noticed how communication everywhere is breaking down? Have you noticed how we are, as far as communication goes, living in the age of the absurd—where everything offends somebody somewhere at some point? No matter what you say, it's almost impossible to not offend somebody as you speak.

I wonder what the legacy of our time will be. I wonder what history books will say fifty or one hundred years from now when they look back at what's going on today and start commenting on how people conducted themselves in communication and language. I have a feeling that the current rhetoric is not going to be seen as a good thing.

One thing, however, that our age has made abundantly clear—and this actually confirms what the scriptures taught before—is that words are incredibly powerful. Think about God Himself and think about the way that He chose to reveal His Son to us. In the Gospel of John, the very first title given to the Son is "the Word."“ The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.” And sometimes we don't realize that language is not a human invention. God talked to Himself prior to creation. So language is a characteristic of God Himself that He shares with us.

Language also predates the fall. God talked to Adam before sin entered. So communication, language, is something that's been around forever and is a need that we have—we're communicating people, communing people, speaking to one another. It is of utmost importance.

But we also know that communication and language have been marked by sin. As a matter of fact, the very way that Satan tempted Eve was through the misuse of language. Remember what the temptation is in Genesis chapter three? "Did God really say? No, you're not going to die. God said you're going to die, but He didn't mean that."

Satan himself uses miscommunication to bring sin into this world. When married couples come to me for counseling, the primary presentation problem—the thing that they say they have a problem with—is almost one hundred percent of the time communication. And as you start to work with them, sometimes it becomes clear that they actually have no problem communicating what they want. That's really what they come from.

## The Root of Communication Problems

Communication problems are not primarily the way that words come out of our mouths. That's not the primary focus. The Lord Jesus Christ said in Matthew chapter twelve that it's out of the heart that the man speaks. It is out of the heart that the issues of life come. That's why in Proverbs 4:23, Solomon tells us that we have to guard the heart, for out of it spring all the issues of life. Communication issues are actually not word issues but heart issues. It is primarily an issue that flows from our desires ruling our hearts.

Who is the ruler of your heart? The clearest way of knowing that is by looking at how we communicate with those around us. A life of godly communication is rooted in a personal recognition of the sovereignty of God. The sovereignty of God is one of the most wonderful doctrines of the scriptures, and every doctrine must be applied to life—including the way that we talk to one another.

Only when we submit to the rule of God through faith in Jesus Christ, who has a perfect plan and is in complete control, will we begin to live and speak as He has purposed for us. And really, only at this level—when Christ is ruling over our hearts—will monstrous words be removed from our mouths. As long as Christ is not king of our hearts, we're going to continue to be idolaters, and that's going to be manifest in the way that we talk.

It is here alone, in the sovereignty of God through Christ Jesus over our hearts, that we are freed from being the tools of our own agenda. How often we use our words to control people around us, to control our agenda, to establish and do what we want to do. How often we use our words to attempt to control people around us, or to seek glory for ourselves.

Only when Christ is ruling over our hearts, and we are worshiping Him and Him alone, will we get over those things. When our hearts are controlled by desires for the things of creation—for power, for love, for relationships, or whatever it is—we will seek to control everything around us, and we're going to use our language to do just that. But when we understand God's sovereignty and submit to His rule, we can live and speak as God has designed.

God's revealed will for us is that all our speaking be done for the praise of His glory. So a firm understanding and belief in the sovereignty of God will propel us onto God's communication. That's really what we're going to spend the rest of the time understanding—God's sovereignty as it applies to God's communication.

## Seven Statements on God's Sovereignty and Communication

When the Bible says that God is sovereign, it is saying that He rules the universe unchallenged. I think there are at least seven applications of that to communication.

### Statement 1: God Is Lord of Lords and King of Kings

No one gives Him advice. No one can legitimately question Him. No one can stand in the way of His will. Listen to what Nebuchadnezzar found out after spending seven years walking around the field as a cow, eating grass, because he refused to recognize the sovereignty of God. Nebuchadnezzar says in Daniel chapter four:

*"At the end of the time, I lifted my eyes to heaven and my understanding returned to me. And I blessed the most high and praised and honored Him who lives forever. For His dominion is an everlasting dominion. And His kingdom is from generation to generation. All the inhabitants of the earth are reputed as nothing. He does according to His will in the army of heaven and among the inhabitants of the earth. No one can restrain His hand or say to Him, 'What have you done?'"*

Now how does this idea apply to communication? This way: we will never be in a situation, location, or relationship in which God is not ruling. You're never going to be in a place where God is not ruling over that. You're never going to be in a situation where God is not ruling over that. And knowing that God is in control of our lives allows us not to give in to panic, not to give in to anxiety. We can pay attention to what God has called us to do, to live and speak for His glory in whatever situation that we are in.

### Statement 2: God Rules Over All Things for the Church

God rules for the redemptive benefit of His people. God has worked and is working all things in history in order to gather people for Himself to the praise of His own glorious name. Look at Ephesians 1:7-12:

*"In Him we have redemption through His blood, the forgiveness of sins according to the riches of His grace, which He made to abound to us in all wisdom and prudence. He made known to us the mystery of His will according to His good pleasure, which He purposed in Himself, that in the dispensation of the fullness of the times, He might gather together in one all things in Christ, both which are in heaven and which are on earth. In Him also we have obtained an inheritance, being predestined according to the purpose of Him who works all things according to the counsel of His will, that we who first trusted in Christ should be to the praise of His glory."*

God is working everything to bring people to Himself. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, the one who became incarnate like we are, who lived the perfect life, who died the death we deserve, who rose again from the dead—is our King today. Not just in the future, but right now.

When you think of communication in light of God giving Christ to be the head of the church, think about how much of our communication involves complaining about our circumstances. How much of what we say involves complaining about our circumstances—those very circumstances that God has been working before the foundation of the world to bring you into the church of Jesus Christ?

What is it that you praise God for? So much of our praise of the Lord is limited to the moments when we have determined that what He has done is good. We praise the Lord for times of physical healing, financial provision, restored relationships, solved problems. And in those situations we praise the Lord for His faithfulness. But in the other situations, somehow He's not being faithful to His church.

All this communication often reflects the fact that God is active in every moment of our lives. When we believe that this is the case, we have hearts that can speak with humility and worship.

### Statement 3: God Rules Over the Specific Details of Our Lives

God rules not just globally or universally—He rules over the very specific details of our lives. God's sovereignty is not just universal but individual. If you read the words of Paul in Acts chapter seventeen, where He's preaching to the Athenian philosophers, He says that God appointed the place where you live, the family you're in—and God is near you so that you can reach out to Him.

When you think of communication in light of that, we may have communication problems over things we think of as problems, but God doesn't. People tend to focus on the people around them when communication problems arise. In the midst of a fight between a husband and wife, the husband doesn't usually scream angrily at the ceiling. That's not how it goes. But God is using the things in our lives as tools for accomplishing His work in us, because He is near us. That should shape the way we communicate.

### Statement 4: God Rules Over Every Aspect of Our Salvation

Look at Ephesians 1:3-6:

*"Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ who has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ, just as He chose us in Him before the foundation of the world that we should be holy and without blame before Him in love, having predestined us to adoption as sons by Jesus Christ to Himself according to the good pleasure of His will, to the praise of the glory of His grace, with which He made us accepted in the Beloved."*

God has done everything that has to be done for our salvation, for our redemption. That's the only reason we can have assurance of salvation—because if we could lose it, we would. God rules supreme over our salvation.

That's really where true worship begins. True worship begins when we grasp the sovereign grace of God. God's sovereign grace undermines all human pride and thoughts of self-sufficiency, as we face our complete dependence on Him. As we grasp sovereign grace, the pride we would always have gives way to praising Him.

How does this relate to communication? The highest and first goal of all our communication is that our words would reflect an attitude of worship that recognizes our utter dependence on God for our salvation. Is every word and phrase marked by your realizing your utter need of Christ in your salvation? He has chosen us to be His children. He caused Himself to breathe life into us so that we could see and believe the truth. He justified us and adopted us into His family. In our realization of that, there will come a life of communication that benefits others and honors Him.

### Statement 5: God Rules Over Circumstances for Our Sanctification

God is at work in every situation to conform us to the image of His Son. Romans 8:28-29 says that He's working all things for good for those who are called according to His purpose, so that they may be conformed to the image of His Son.

If this is the case, then when we complain about the problems and pressures of our lives, we are essentially grumbling in the face of God. If God is working everything in our life to sanctify us, to shape us to be more like Christ, and we complain about those things—we are complaining about the plan of God.

Our circumstances shout to us—sometimes telling us that God has forgotten us. But really, every situation in life is telling us that God has remembered us and will not leave us till the work is complete. That's why James could say, "Count it all joy when you fall into various trials, knowing that the testing of your faith produces patience."

The texting neighbor, the demanding boss, the touchy relative, the controlling friend, the unthankful child, the unexpected accident—are all tools of sanctification in the hands of our Lord. When we're going through things, we realize that even those things are coming from the hand of a good God who is shaping us to be more like His Son. Is that showing up in the way that we relate and talk to each other?

### Statement 6: God Rules Over Relationships for My Sanctification

The people in our lives are not there by accident. Through them, God continues the work He has begun in us. Ephesians chapter two talks about how He's bringing all people together in Jesus Christ. Ephesians chapter four says that we are equipping one another so that together we can come into the fullness of the Head, the Lord Jesus Christ.

We often forget that God places people in our lives in order to shape us in the image of Christ. We treat them as irritants or obstacles. The untimely phone call, the demand to do something for them, the person who we see as annoying—we see them as people to get rid of. But we need to see them as people that God brought into our lives to shape us, to make us like Christ.

Instead we lash out in anger because they stand in the way of our plans, our momentary happiness. Embracing the sovereignty of God over our relationships and circumstances will alter our words regarding the people He has placed in our lives. Can you think of one annoying person in your life this week? That person is in your life by the hand of God. God sent that person as a messenger to help you become more like Jesus Christ.

### Statement 7: God Rules Over All Things for His Glory

Look at Ephesians 1:12: "That we who first trusted in Christ should be to the praise of His glory."

People of God: God is not working for your temporal happiness. God is not working for your best life now. If your best life is now, you're going to hell. For the believer, your best life is to come at the resurrection of Jesus Christ. He is not working right now so that we would feel satisfied and complete, or that we would have a positive self-image or a comfortable lifestyle. He is working to make us lights that shine in darkness so that people will see our good works and give Him glory.

Bottom line: life is not about you. Life is not about me. Life is about God glorifying Himself through us. As our Lord Jesus said, "Let your light so shine before men that they may see your good works and glorify your Father in heaven."

When we speak as if we are in control, when we take glory for ourselves, when we complain about the things God has put on our plate, we are rejecting His ultimate purpose for us—that we would be people who live and speak for His glory.

In light of this, every word we speak must meet two standards: all our words should bring God the glory He deserves, and all our words should bring redemptive good into the lives of the people who God has placed around us.

Is this whoever hears you and me speak should be closer to Jesus? That's the redemptive purpose in our communication. When we speak, the grace of God is imparted to people. That's the goal of communication.

## Seven Questions for Self-Examination

As we finish here, let me ask you seven questions. I'm just going to ask them—not all comment on them—but I want you to take them to heart.

1. In what ways is your communication an attempt to take control of all things?
2. How would the way you communicate be different if you believed and trusted more fully in God's absolute control of everything for your good and His glory?
3. How do you respond when God sends suffering or disappointment your way?
4. Do you seek to speak in a way that encourages the work God is doing in others?
5. Do you treat certain people as obstacles to your own spiritual growth? Or do you see them as God's agents of your sanctification and as the intended recipients of God's grace through you?
6. (Implied) How do you treat people who seem to stand in the way of what you want?
7. How specifically do your words reveal whether you are resting in God's control or wrestling with it?

The Lord Jesus is the sovereign King of the universe, and the universe includes the way we communicate. We want—I hope that's our desire—a society where people actually understand each other, where people can talk to one another and be better for that, growing together through communication. And that society begins as we, the Church of Jesus Christ, speak in a way consistent with the truth that He is the Lord of everything.

Do you want to change the society we live in? We start right here in the church, reflecting the sovereignty of God in all things.

*Father, we thank You that You are God who speaks, thank You that You have given us Your Word to point us to Christ, and we thank You that You have given us each other that we might point each other to Christ and be pointed to Christ. Help us to speak according to the truth that our Lord Jesus is the Lord of Lords and King of Kings. And we pray Father, if there is any here today who have not come under the acknowledgment that God is King and that Jesus is their Savior, that You save them even today. In Jesus' name. Amen.*
